patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling really tired lately, and my eyes look a little yellow sometimes.” Also volunteer early: you get short of breath faster on stairs, urine looks darker some mornings, and your skin seems pale. If asked about pain or other symptoms, mention a dull ache/fullness under your left ribs, feeling wiped out after colds/stomach bugs, easier bruising, and that a doctor once said your smear had “bite cells” but you didn’t understand. Do not mention unless specifically asked: gallbladder-type RUQ pain after greasy meals, being told your spleen was enlarged, cold/blue hands/feet in cold, or that mild yellowing has happened on and off for years with stress/illness. If asked about relationships/sex, admit you’ve avoided intimacy due to embarrassment and fear. Express misconceptions: you tried iron supplements without benefit; you worried about hepatitis and kidney problems. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share financial worries about genetic testing, frequent labs/specialists, and missing work.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (hemolytic anemia due to RBC membrane permeability defect)
required_evidence: Evidence of hemolysis on CBC indices pattern and reticulocytosis (elevated reticulocyte count/absolute reticulocyte count) consistent with compensated hemolysis
RBC indices compatible with stomatocytosis phenotype (often macrocytosis with low MCHC in overhydrated form; may be high MCHC in dehydrated form/xerocytosis)

Peripheral smear finding consistent with abnormal RBC morphology (stomatocytes may be present; note that 'bite cells' suggests oxidant hemolysis and should trigger consideration of G6PD deficiency as a differential)
No medication exposure history suggesting oxidant drug-induced hemolysis and no features provided to support immune hemolysis

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low to mildly low (anemia of variable severity) consistent with chronic hemolysis
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Low to mildly low, proportional to hemoglobin
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low or low-normal (may be relatively preserved if compensated)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to elevated (macrocytosis common in overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis; can be normal/high in xerocytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCHC: Low in overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis; normal to high in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis (xerocytosis)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
non_pharmacological: Avoid splenectomy unless strongly indicated and managed by hematology (in hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis, splenectomy can increase thromboembolic risk)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia or rapid clinical worsening

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane condition that can cause chronic hemolysis (breakdown of red cells) and anemia

Acknowledge that 'bite cells' can also suggest G6PD deficiency and that confirmatory evaluation is needed

Treatment is mainly supportive; transfusion is reserved for severe symptomatic anemia
Splenectomy is generally avoided/used cautiously due to increased clot risk in hereditary stomatocytosis/xerocytosis
Discuss expected course and when to seek urgent care (worsening fatigue, shortness of breath, chest pain, syncope, jaundice, dark urine)
Discuss family implications and the potential need for family member evaluation
Maintain good hydration and nutrition; adhere to folic acid
Avoid unnecessary medications that could worsen hemolysis until diagnosis is confirmed; review new meds with clinician
Ensure clinicians are aware of penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) before antibiotics are prescribed

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for hemolysis/anemia, triggers, family history, prior labs, transfusions, thrombotic history, pregnancy status where relevant, and medication exposures.

Review lab results for evidence of hemolysis (reticulocytosis, elevated indirect bilirubin/LDH, low haptoglobin) and smear findings; identify alternative diagnoses requiring confirmatory testing (e.g., G6PD deficiency).

Meet required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, supportive treatment approach, splenectomy caution, urgent return precautions, family implications, and lifestyle/medication guidance including penicillin allergy disclosure.
If indicated, prescribe folic acid after allergy check; avoid unnecessary meds that may worsen hemolysis until diagnosis is confirmed.
Arrange follow-up for review of confirmatory testing, monitoring anemia/hemolysis, and referral (hematology) if needed.
Close encounter after safety-netting, ensuring patient understands plan and when to seek urgent care.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman who is anxious and a bit vague. Start with: “I keep getting these sudden hot flashes where my face turns bright red and I feel awful.” Early on, volunteer: sudden flushing of face/upper chest, watery diarrhea several times daily, wheezing/tight chest during spells, and that you were told you have a heart murmur. If asked, describe episodes lasting a few minutes, happening multiple times/day, with pounding heartbeat, weight loss without trying, belly cramping before diarrhea, triggers (alcohol, stress, spicy foods), and more shortness of breath climbing stairs.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: fatigue, ankle puffiness, lightheadedness on standing, worse diarrhea after big meals/avoiding eating, warm blotchy skin during attacks.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 39-year-old woman, vague and evasive. Start with: “I can’t stop these nosebleeds, and I’m covered in bruises for no reason.” Volunteer only: recurrent hard-to-stop nosebleeds for 1 week, unexplained bruises, gum bleeding with brushing, and feeling wiped out/weak for weeks. Minimize details unless pressed; say you’re “not sure” or “it’s probably nothing.” If asked directly, admit: non-blanching tiny red/purple dots on ankles/legs, short of breath on stairs, low-grade fevers/chills, dizziness on standing, heavier periods this month, a new/worse headache today, and one episode of pink urine. Do not mention mouth sores, early fullness, paleness, racing heart, or intermittent pleuritic chest tightness unless specifically asked about those symptoms. Resist/avoid: post-sex bleeding, frequent ibuprofen use, and “blood-thinning” herbal supplements; only reveal if asked nonjudgmentally. Express misconceptions: you’ve always bruised easily, family “bleeding problems,” maybe hemophilia, dentist caused gum bleeding, heavy periods are “normal.” When shown empathy, soften briefly, then shift to fears about missing work, insurance, costs, and ask to keep diagnosis off paperwork; consider leaving unless reassured about urgency and help with costs.

confounders: recent heavy menstrual bleeding and iron supplementation history leading to attribution of fatigue/bruising to gynecologic causes
intermittent ibuprofen use increasing mucosal bleeding risk in setting of thrombocytopenia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), PML-RARA positive (suspected)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute myeloid leukemia with APL morphology pending confirmation
required_evidence: Peripheral smear showing abnormal promyelocytes with heavy granulation and/or Auer rods (often bundles/faggot cells)
Thrombocytopenia with anemia (often with leukopenia or leukocytosis)
Coagulopathy pattern consistent with DIC/consumptive coagulopathy (PT/INR prolonged; aPTT may be normal or prolonged)
Clinical urgency: suspected APL is treated immediately with ATRA without waiting for cytogenetic confirmation

expected_lab_results.WBC: Variable; often low-normal or elevated (can be <10k or markedly high in high-risk disease)
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Often low or functionally abnormal; may be reduced
expected_lab_results.Peripheral smear: Promyelocyte predominance with hypergranular blasts and Auer rods; possible faggot cells
expected_lab_results.PT/INR: Prolonged (evidence of consumptive coagulopathy/DIC common in APL)
expected_lab_results.aPTT: Normal to prolonged (may be mildly prolonged in DIC)

medications: All-trans retinoic acid (ATRA, tretinoin) 45 mg/m^2/day PO in 2 divided doses (start immediately on suspicion; continue through induction per protocol)
Arsenic trioxide 0.15 mg/kg IV daily (induction for non–high-risk APL per protocol; if high-risk add cytoreductive agent per hematology)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12h (start at first signs of differentiation syndrome; consider early if high WBC or symptoms per hematology)
Platelet transfusion to maintain platelets >50 x10^9/L (higher threshold if active bleeding or procedures)
Fresh frozen plasma to target PT/INR normalization and treat bleeding/consumptive coagulopathy (per institutional DIC protocol)
Cryoprecipitate to maintain fibrinogen >150 mg/dL (if fibrinogen low; commonly required in APL-associated coagulopathy)
Hydroxyurea 1-3 g/day PO in divided doses for cytoreduction if leukocytosis (per hematology; dose titrated to WBC)
non_pharmacological: Immediate hematology/oncology consult and admit to monitored setting (high risk of fatal hemorrhage early in APL)
Avoid intramuscular injections and minimize invasive procedures; use gentle venipuncture
Frequent monitoring (at least q6-8h initially): CBC with differential and coagulation parameters; monitor for bleeding and differentiation syndrome
Baseline and serial ECG and electrolytes (K, Mg) if arsenic trioxide is used; maintain K >4.0 mEq/L and Mg >2.0 mg/dL to reduce QT risk
If febrile neutropenia suspected, initiate guideline-based empiric antibiotics that avoid penicillin class due to allergy (select per local protocol and severity)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate initiation of ATRA and supportive transfusions; reassess labs and bleeding status within 4-6 hours, then at least every 6-8 hours during early induction; daily hematology-directed review

must_include: Suspected APL is a medical emergency because of a high risk of life-threatening bleeding from a clotting disorder
Diagnosis is suggested by the blood smear and labs; confirmatory genetic testing (PML-RARA) will be sent but treatment starts immediately
ATRA should be started right away even before confirmation because early treatment reduces bleeding-related death
Induction therapy typically includes ATRA plus arsenic trioxide (and additional agents if high-risk) under hematology supervision
Supportive care includes aggressive transfusion support to correct low platelets and abnormal clotting tests
Key serious complication: differentiation syndrome—review warning signs (fever, weight gain, breathing trouble, low blood pressure) and need for urgent steroids
Discuss bleeding precautions and why procedures/IM injections are avoided
Review penicillin allergy and ensure it is documented for any antibiotics or other medications
Set expectations for ICU/step-down monitoring and frequent blood draws early in treatment
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and other agents that increase bleeding risk unless specifically directed
Seek immediate help for any new bleeding, severe headache/neurologic symptoms, chest pain, or shortness of breath
Infection precautions if neutropenic (hand hygiene, report fever promptly)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit acute bleeding symptoms, infection symptoms, neurologic/respiratory symptoms, medication history (including anticoagulants/antiplatelets/NSAIDs), prior malignancy/chemo, pregnancy status, and confirm penicillin allergy details; establish time course and severity to frame APL as an emergency.

Order immediate APL-focused workup and hemorrhagic risk assessment: CBC with differential, peripheral smear review, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer, CMP (electrolytes/renal/hepatic), uric acid, LDH, type and screen/crossmatch; send confirmatory PML-RARA testing (FISH/PCR) but do not delay treatment.

Interpret results for suspected APL pattern (promyelocytes/blasts, thrombocytopenia, anemia, coagulopathy with low fibrinogen/elevated D-dimer) and assess immediate bleeding/DIC risk to justify emergent therapy and transfusion support.
Document suspected acute promyelocytic leukemia as a medical emergency and explicitly note that treatment should begin immediately while confirmatory PML-RARA testing is pending.

Perform and document an allergy check prior to any prescribing; specifically confirm and record penicillin allergy and reaction type/severity to guide antibiotic selection during neutropenia or infection.

Review drug-drug interactions relevant to APL induction/supportive care (e.g., QT-prolonging agents with arsenic trioxide, azoles/macrolides/fluoroquinolones, anticoagulants/antiplatelets/NSAIDs increasing bleeding). This is non-critical but expected.

Initiate immediate ATRA for suspected APL (do not wait for confirmation) and order/arrange induction under hematology supervision (ATRA + arsenic trioxide; consider additional agents if high-risk). Ensure aggressive transfusion support orders/plan (platelets, cryoprecipitate/FFP as indicated) and monitoring; avoid IM injections/procedures when possible.

Communicate diagnosis urgency, rationale for immediate ATRA, induction plan, supportive transfusions, bleeding precautions, differentiation syndrome warning signs and need for urgent steroids, avoidance of aspirin/NSAIDs, when to seek emergency help, infection precautions if neutropenic, and expectations for ICU/step-down monitoring and frequent labs.

Arrange immediate hematology/oncology admission-level care (ICU/step-down) and ensure ongoing monitoring plan (serial CBC/coags/fibrinogen, electrolytes/QT monitoring if ATO) with clear escalation instructions.
Close after confirming understanding, documenting safety checks, and ensuring urgent disposition and treatment initiation.

minimal_paths.description: Standard optimal path: recognize suspected APL from presentation/labs, start ATRA immediately after safety checks, coordinate induction and transfusion support, and provide differentiation syndrome/bleeding education with urgent admission.

Regular insulin IV infusion 0.1 units/kg/hour (start after confirming K+ >=3.3 mmol/L; consider 0.1 units/kg IV bolus only if local protocol)

Dextrose 5% in 0.45% sodium chloride IV when glucose reaches ~200 mg/dL, continue insulin until anion gap closes

Strict intake/output monitoring; assess volume status and mental status
Frequent lab reassessment (BMP/anion gap) every 2-4 hours during acute management
Identify and treat precipitating cause (e.g., infection, missed insulin, MI) based on history/exam and additional tests
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess electrolytes and anion gap every 2-4 hours during treatment; transition to subcutaneous insulin when anion gap closed and patient able to eat; outpatient follow-up within 1 week after discharge (or earlier if new diagnosis).

must_include: Body is producing acids (ketones) due to insufficient insulin, causing dangerous dehydration and electrolyte shifts
This is an emergency requiring IV fluids, insulin, and close monitoring
Explain what anion gap and low bicarbonate mean in plain language (blood is too acidic)
IV fluids first to treat dehydration and protect kidneys
Insulin given by IV to stop ketone production and lower glucose safely
Potassium and other electrolytes will be checked often and replaced as needed; insulin can make potassium drop
Dextrose will be added later even though glucose is high to allow insulin to continue until acidosis resolves
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent care (vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, confusion, inability to keep fluids down)
Clarify expected monitoring and potential complications (low potassium, low blood sugar, fluid overload in at-risk patients)

nl_assertions: Agent recognizes DKA as a medical emergency requiring ED/ICU-level monitoring and does not attempt outpatient management.

Agent explains DKA pathophysiology in plain language: insufficient insulin leads to ketone acid build-up causing dehydration and dangerous electrolyte shifts; connects this to anion gap and low bicarbonate meaning the blood is too acidic.

Agent explains treatment sequencing: IV fluids first, then IV insulin; frequent electrolyte checks and replacement, emphasizing insulin can lower potassium; adds dextrose later to continue insulin until acidosis resolves (anion gap closes) even if glucose is improving.
Agent performs allergy_check before prescribing and documents penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and reassures that core DKA meds (IV fluids, insulin, electrolytes) do not require penicillin.

Agent performs contraindication_check conceptually before prescribing insulin/fluids/electrolytes (e.g., do not start insulin if potassium is dangerously low; adjust fluids in heart/renal failure risk; avoid bicarbonate unless severe acidemia).
Agent uses lab results to choose potassium strategy: if K+ <3.3, hold insulin and replete K+; if 3.3–5.2, give K+ with insulin; if >5.2, start insulin without K+ but monitor closely.

Agent includes monitoring expectations and potential complications: hypokalemia, hypoglycemia, cerebral edema (especially pediatrics), fluid overload in at-risk patients, and need for frequent reassessment.
Agent addresses warning signs requiring urgent care: vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid/deep breathing, confusion, inability to keep fluids down, worsening weakness or dehydration.

Agent provides sick-day rules: never stop basal insulin, check glucose and ketones more often, maintain hydration and carbohydrate intake as able, seek care early; assesses adherence and access barriers to insulin/supplies.

Agent avoids unsafe medication suggestions (e.g., SGLT2 inhibitors during/after DKA without careful specialist input; sedatives/opioids for abdominal pain without evaluation; inappropriate antibiotics without infection evidence).

steps: ASK: assess symptoms, triggers, severity, and immediate safety
GET_RESULTS: confirm anion gap metabolic acidosis with ketones and hyperglycemia; review K+/renal function
DIAGNOSE: DKA; explain anion gap/low bicarbonate in plain language and emergency nature
CHECK_INTERACTION: screen home meds for potassium/glucose interactions
PRESCRIBE: IV isotonic fluids first; potassium plan based on K+; IV insulin; electrolyte monitoring/repletion; add dextrose when glucose decreases while continuing insulin until gap closes
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis, treatment, monitoring/complications, warning signs; sick-day rules; adherence/access barriers
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: diabetes education and outpatient follow-up after stabilization

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I’ve been extremely tired and weak ever since I had my baby.” Volunteer these early: lightheaded on standing like you might faint, feeling cold all the time, and that your periods haven’t returned since delivery. If asked about breastfeeding, sexual function, bowels, skin/hair, or low blood sugar, reveal: you couldn’t make enough milk, libido is basically gone, more constipation, drier skin and thinner hair, and occasional shaky/sweaty weakness when you miss meals. Do not mention unless specifically asked about delivery complications, bleeding, transfusion, body hair changes, thirst/urination, or concentration: heavy bleeding needing transfusion, sparse underarm/pubic hair, less thirst and peeing less, and slowed thinking. Be reluctant about urinary leakage and especially painful/dry sex—minimize and say it’s embarrassing. Suggest you think it might be postpartum depression or iron deficiency; note iron and energy drinks haven’t helped much. Express cost worries about “optional” hormone tests/MRI, meds, and missing work. If the clinician shows empathy, accept it briefly and stay engaged.

medications: Testosterone gel 1% 5 g topically once daily
comorbidities: History of severe postpartum hemorrhage with hypotension (2 years prior)
confounders: Postpartum depression and chronic fatigue symptoms overlapping endocrine insufficiency
Iron-deficiency anemia in the postpartum period (fatigue/lightheadedness may be attributed to anemia)
Intentional weight-loss dieting with intermittent fasting (can contribute to hypoglycemia and fatigue)
Recent initiation of testosterone therapy (may mask hypoestrogen symptoms and alter libido/energy perception)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hypopituitarism with secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism)
acceptable_alternatives: Hypopituitarism (likely postpartum) with secondary adrenal insufficiency
Secondary adrenal insufficiency due to pituitary failure
Central hypothyroidism due to pituitary failure
required_evidence: Low 8 AM serum cortisol with low or inappropriately normal ACTH (secondary adrenal insufficiency pattern)
Low free T4 with low or inappropriately normal TSH (central hypothyroidism pattern)
Low prolactin (supports pituitary lactotroph failure) or inappropriately low prolactin for postpartum context
Associated metabolic abnormalities consistent with adrenal insufficiency and/or hypothyroidism (e.g., hyponatremia, low/normal glucose)
Clinical context compatible with postpartum pituitary infarction/necrosis (e.g., history of severe postpartum hemorrhage, failure to lactate, amenorrhea—if elicited)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low (hyponatremia) consistent with secondary adrenal insufficiency and/or hypothyroidism
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal (hyperkalemia is not expected in secondary adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): Low to low-normal (risk of hypoglycemia in adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (not elevated) consistent with secondary adrenal insufficiency
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal despite low free T4 (central hypothyroidism)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg PO daily in divided doses (e.g., 10 mg AM, 5 mg mid-afternoon, +/- 5 mg early evening)
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start only after glucocorticoid replacement is initiated; consider lower starting dose 25-50 mcg daily if older/cardiac risk)
non_pharmacological: Initiate glucocorticoid replacement before thyroid hormone to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
Provide adrenal insufficiency sick-day rules (increase hydrocortisone during febrile illness; emergency dosing plan)
Prescribe/educate on medical alert identification for adrenal insufficiency
Arrange endocrinology follow-up and evaluation for additional pituitary hormone deficits (gonadotropins, GH) and pituitary MRI as appropriate
Review current testosterone use for indication and safety; avoid using testosterone as a substitute for appropriate pituitary hormone replacement
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for symptom review and dose adjustment; sooner (24-72 hours) if symptomatic hypotension, vomiting, severe fatigue, or concern for adrenal crisis

must_include: Condition is due to pituitary hormone deficiency (often after postpartum pituitary injury) leading to low cortisol and low thyroid hormone
Low cortisol is the most urgent deficiency because it can cause adrenal crisis
Explain how labs support pituitary (secondary) causes: low cortisol with low/normal ACTH and low free T4 with low/normal TSH
Start hydrocortisone first; then start levothyroxine after steroid replacement is in place
How to take hydrocortisone (divided dosing; do not stop abruptly)
Sick-day rules and when to seek emergency care (vomiting, inability to keep pills down, severe weakness, fainting, confusion)
Need for ongoing monitoring and possible additional hormone replacement depending on full pituitary evaluation
Discuss testosterone use: confirm indication, potential adverse effects, and that it does not treat cortisol/thyroid deficiencies
Review allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) and reassure that prescribed hormones are not penicillin-related
Discuss fertility/menstrual or postpartum concerns if relevant and plan for endocrine/gynecology input
Carry medical alert identification and an emergency steroid plan
Avoid missed doses; have an extra supply for travel
Encourage prompt evaluation for intercurrent illness and adherence to follow-up labs/appointments

Pain distribution consistent with pudendal nerve territory (vulva/perineum, possibly rectal/peri-anal; may include dyspareunia)
No objective evidence of infection/systemic inflammation on available labs (normal/near-normal WBC, CRP, ESR)

Exclusion of common mimics based on history/exam: no vesicular lesions (HSV), no abnormal discharge/odor (vaginitis), no focal vulvar dermatosis suspicious for lichen sclerosus/planus, no red-flag neurologic deficits
Consider supportive exam findings when present: tenderness over ischial spine/Alcock canal; reproduction of pain with palpation; relief with diagnostic pudendal nerve block (if performed)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no anemia contributing to fatigue/pain amplification)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal or minimally elevated (not suggestive of acute infection/inflammatory disease)
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Creatinine: Within reference range (needed before/while using renally cleared neuropathic agents)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or mildly elevated; if elevated, note diabetes as a neuropathy risk modifier but not primary cause

medications: Gabapentin 100 mg PO at bedtime for 3 days, then 100 mg PO twice daily for 3 days, then 100 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 300 mg PO three times daily (max per tolerance/renal function)
Lidocaine 5% ointment apply thin layer to painful vulvar area nightly or 20-30 minutes before provocation (e.g., sitting/intercourse) as needed
non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy focusing on pelvic floor relaxation, myofascial release, and down-training (avoid strengthening/Kegels unless specifically indicated)
Activity modification: avoid prolonged sitting; use a perineal cut-out cushion; frequent standing breaks
Avoid mechanical triggers (cycling/rower/spinning) until improved; gradual return with ergonomic adjustments
Vulvar care: gentle cleansing with water, avoid irritants (scented soaps, douches), use bland emollients as barrier if needed
Consider referral to pain medicine/gynecology/urology for diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block and multidisciplinary chronic pain management if inadequate response
Screen and address comorbid anxiety/depression/sleep disturbance; consider CBT or pain psychology
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for response/tolerability and dose titration; earlier (within 3-7 days) if adverse effects or worsening symptoms

must_include: Pain is real and can be neuropathic (nerve-related) even when labs are normal
Pudendal neuralgia involves irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve supplying the vulvar/perineal area
Common pattern: worse with sitting, better when off-loading pressure
Diagnosis is clinical and based on symptoms/exam and ruling out other causes; may be supported by response to nerve block
Multimodal plan: pelvic floor PT + nerve-pain medication titration + topical anesthetic + trigger avoidance
Gabapentin is titrated slowly to reduce sedation/dizziness; do not stop abruptly without guidance
Topical lidocaine: how to apply, expected numbness/temporary relief, stop if significant irritation
Review allergies: sulfonamide antibiotics (TMP-SMX) causing urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis—avoid latex gloves/condoms and use non-latex alternatives
Safety counseling: sedation/fall risk with gabapentin; avoid alcohol/other sedatives; caution driving until effects known
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: new weakness/numbness in legs, bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever, rapidly worsening pain, new vulvar lesions/bleeding
Sitting modifications and use of a cut-out cushion; frequent micro-breaks
Avoid high-pressure pelvic activities (cycling) during flare; gradual return with ergonomics
Vulvar skin care and avoidance of irritants; lubrication strategies if sexually active and pain-limited

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I can’t stop gaining weight, and I’m hungry all the time no matter what I eat.” Volunteer: ongoing weight gain despite trying to watch food, constant hunger/food thoughts, low energy/tiring easily. Be embarrassed and initially minimize secret eating/stealing food; admit only if asked gently and privately. Mention partner’s report of jerking/stiffening in sleep and waking confused; don’t insist it’s epilepsy. Express worries: genetic diagnosis could raise insurance costs, testing/specialists will cost thousands, employer might find out and fire you. Non-critical but expected before prescribing; assess interactions (e.g., tranexamic acid with combined hormonal contraceptives/other prothrombotic meds; DDAVP with SSRIs/carbamazepine/thiazides increasing hyponatremia risk).

Arrange hematology follow-up, provide written emergency/procedure plan, and ensure patient has medical alert/bleeding disorder card.
Close encounter after confirming understanding and safety-netting.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, tangential project coordinator. Start with: “I just can’t sleep like I used to—most nights I’m up for hours.” Volunteer: trouble falling asleep (takes 1–2+ hours), frequent awakenings, unrefreshed mornings, 2 months duration, affecting daytime function. Ramble a bit (work stress, schedules), then come back to sleep.

If asked, add: early-morning awakening, tired/foggy/irritable, clock-checking and worry about sleep, weekend naps, afternoon coffee, tried melatonin/OTC aids with little help.

Do NOT offer unless specifically asked: phone scrolling in bed 1–2 hours, nocturia 1–2x/night, weight gain/tight neck, jumpy legs needing movement, alcohol to fall asleep, nighttime heartburn.

Express misconceptions: worry about thyroid/hormones/ADHD, “addicted to stress.” Emphasize cost concerns; prefer a single prescription. If clinician shows empathy, soften, say you feel heard, and share one extra detail.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia)
acceptable_alternatives: Insomnia secondary to restless legs syndrome due to iron deficiency
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating and/or maintaining sleep or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, impaired concentration/mood, reduced functioning)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week and persists at least 3 months (if chronic); specify duration if shorter
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep are present
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder (e.g., sleep apnea, circadian rhythm disorder) or untreated medical/psychiatric condition
Medication review identifies Orphenadrine use and assesses temporal relationship to insomnia
Screen for RLS symptoms (urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening, relief with movement) and correlate with iron studies

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (rules out hyperthyroidism as a contributor if normal)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Within reference range (rules out hyperthyroidism as a contributor if normal)
expected_lab_results.CBC (Hemoglobin): Normal to mildly low if iron deficiency is present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CBC (MCV): Normal to low (microcytosis supports iron deficiency; normal does not exclude early deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low (<50 ng/mL supports iron deficiency relevant to restless legs syndrome; <30 ng/mL strongly suggests iron deficiency)

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (short-term trial, especially for sleep-onset insomnia)
Doxepin 3-6 mg PO nightly (sleep-maintenance insomnia; avoid if significant anticholinergic burden or contraindications)
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) PO every other day with vitamin C (if ferritin <50 ng/mL or TSAT <20% and RLS symptoms present)
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)

Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, reduce caffeine/alcohol/nicotine (especially afternoon/evening), avoid heavy meals late, reduce evening screen exposure, optimize dark/quiet/cool bedroom
Stimulus control: bed for sleep/sex only; if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes, leave bed and return when sleepy
Review Orphenadrine timing/necessity; consider dose timing earlier in day or alternative therapy if contributing to insomnia (coordinate with prescribing clinician)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess sleep metrics (sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, total sleep time), daytime function, side effects, and need for therapy adjustment; sooner (1 week) if significant adverse effects or severe impairment.

must_include: Define insomnia and distinguish acute vs chronic based on duration/frequency

CBT-I is first-line and often more durable than medications

Shared decision-making on whether to use medication vs CBT-I alone
Safety plan for red flags: severe mood symptoms, suicidal ideation, symptoms of sleep apnea (loud snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), or dangerous sleepiness (driving/work hazards)
Concrete sleep schedule plan (fixed wake time; bedtime adjusted by sleep restriction principles)
Caffeine cutoff and alcohol guidance (avoid alcohol as a sleep aid)
Environment optimization and pre-sleep wind-down routine

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia phenotype and duration/frequency (acute vs chronic), sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening, sleep schedule, naps, sleep environment, and daytime impairment.

Screen for contributors: stress/anxiety/depression, pain, nocturia, reflux, substance use (caffeine/alcohol/nicotine/cannabis), shift work/jet lag, and current meds/supplements (explicitly including Orphenadrine timing/dose).

Safety screening for secondary sleep disorders and red flags: OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), RLS symptoms, parasomnias, mania/hypomania, suicidal ideation, and dangerous sleepiness (driving/work).
Order thyroid testing (e.g., TSH ± free T4) and iron studies (ferritin ± iron/TIBC) when clinically indicated to rule out hyperthyroidism and assess iron deficiency/RLS contribution.
Review lab results to refine differential and guide iron repletion plan if ferritin is low or RLS suspected.
Make/confirm insomnia diagnosis (acute vs chronic) and document likely contributing factors (behavioral/schedule/substances/medication effects including Orphenadrine if relevant).

Explain diagnosis and rationale for labs; introduce CBT-I as first-line; provide sleep hygiene plus sleep restriction/stimulus control basics; address Orphenadrine anticholinergic effects and timing considerations.
Before any prescribing, confirm allergies and prior adverse reactions to hypnotics/sedatives (critical safety check).
Before prescribing, check for interactions with current meds/substances (especially other CNS depressants, alcohol, opioids, antihistamines, anticholinergics) (critical safety check).

Explicitly evaluate additive sedation/cognitive impairment risk if Orphenadrine or other anticholinergics/CNS depressants are present (critical safety check; may be combined with prior interaction check if tool supports).

Before prescribing, assess contraindications/precautions: pregnancy, severe hepatic/respiratory disease, history of substance use disorder, falls risk/elderly, OSA risk, severe depression/suicidality, and need for alertness (driving/operating machinery) (critical safety check).

If shared decision-making supports medication, prescribe an appropriate short-term option tailored to sleep-onset vs maintenance and patient risk profile; specify shortest effective duration and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.

Explain treatment plan: CBT-I referral/resources, medication expectations (onset, duration), side effects/precautions, and if iron deficiency/RLS suspected, iron repletion instructions and timeline for improvement.
Arrange follow-up (e.g., 2–4 weeks) to reassess symptoms, adherence to CBT-I/schedule plan, side effects, and lab-guided interventions; earlier follow-up if red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and agreed next steps.

Safety screening for secondary sleep disorders and red flags: OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), RLS symptoms, parasomnias, mania/hypomania, suicidal ideation, and dangerous sleepiness (driving/work).
Order thyroid testing (e.g., TSH ± free T4) and iron studies (ferritin ± iron/TIBC) when clinically indicated to rule out hyperthyroidism and assess iron deficiency/RLS contribution.
Review lab results to refine differential and guide iron repletion plan if ferritin is low or RLS suspected.
Make/confirm insomnia diagnosis (acute vs chronic) and document likely contributing factors (behavioral/schedule/substances/medication effects including Orphenadrine if relevant).

Explain diagnosis and rationale for labs; introduce CBT-I as first-line; provide sleep hygiene plus sleep restriction/stimulus control basics; address Orphenadrine anticholinergic effects and timing considerations.
Before any prescribing, confirm allergies and prior adverse reactions to hypnotics/sedatives (critical safety check).
Before prescribing, check for interactions with current meds/substances (especially other CNS depressants, alcohol, opioids, antihistamines, anticholinergics) (critical safety check).

Explicitly evaluate additive sedation/cognitive impairment risk if Orphenadrine or other anticholinergics/CNS depressants are present (critical safety check; may be combined with prior interaction check if tool supports).

Before prescribing, assess contraindications/precautions: pregnancy, severe hepatic/respiratory disease, history of substance use disorder, falls risk/elderly, OSA risk, severe depression/suicidality, and need for alertness (driving/operating machinery) (critical safety check).

If shared decision-making supports medication, prescribe an appropriate short-term option tailored to sleep-onset vs maintenance and patient risk profile; specify shortest effective duration and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.

Explain treatment plan: CBT-I referral/resources, medication expectations (onset, duration), side effects/precautions, and if iron deficiency/RLS suspected, iron repletion instructions and timeline for improvement.
Arrange follow-up (e.g., 2–4 weeks) to reassess symptoms, adherence to CBT-I/schedule plan, side effects, and lab-guided interventions; earlier follow-up if red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and agreed next steps.

nl_assertions: Agent distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia using duration and frequency criteria and links this to prognosis and management.
Agent characterizes insomnia subtype (sleep-onset, sleep-maintenance, early-morning awakening) and assesses daytime impairment and functional risk (e.g., driving).

Agent screens for common contributors (stress, behaviors, irregular schedule, naps, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine, pain, medical conditions) and reviews medications, explicitly addressing Orphenadrine as a possible contributor.

Agent screens for secondary sleep disorders and red flags: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, mania/hypomania, severe depression/suicidal ideation, and dangerous sleepiness.
Agent explains why thyroid and iron testing are being checked (rule out hyperthyroidism; evaluate iron deficiency/RLS contribution) in clear patient-friendly language.
Agent recommends CBT-I as first-line therapy and explains why it is often more durable than medications.
Agent provides a concrete sleep schedule plan: fixed wake time, sleep restriction principles for bedtime adjustment, and stimulus control guidance.
Agent counsels on caffeine cutoff timing and advises against alcohol as a sleep aid; addresses other sedatives and substance use risks.
Agent discusses environment optimization and a pre-sleep wind-down routine with actionable specifics.
If medication is considered, agent clarifies the goal (sleep onset vs maintenance), expected onset of benefit, and emphasizes the shortest effective duration.

Agent discusses side effects/precautions of sedative-hypnotics: next-day sedation, cognitive impairment, falls risk, complex sleep behaviors (if relevant), and avoidance of combining with alcohol/other sedatives.
Agent elicits patient goals and concerns (dependency, tolerance, daytime grogginess) and uses shared decision-making to choose CBT-I alone vs adding medication.
Agent performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical error.

If iron deficiency/RLS suspected or confirmed, agent explains iron repletion plan, how to take iron to improve absorption (e.g., with vitamin C, away from calcium/antacids), and expected timeline for improvement (weeks).
Agent provides a safety plan/return precautions for red flags: severe mood symptoms or suicidal ideation, symptoms suggestive of sleep apnea, and dangerous sleepiness impacting driving/work.
Agent avoids prescribing when contraindications are present or when untreated high-risk OSA/severe depression/suicidality is suspected; instead prioritizes evaluation/referral and safety.
Agent schedules appropriate follow-up and defines measurable outcomes (sleep latency, wake after sleep onset, total sleep time, daytime function, side effects).

steps: ASK contributors including substances and Orphenadrine

ORDER_LAB TSH ± free T4 and ferritin/iron studies as indicated

minimal_paths.description: Insomnia with suspected/confirmed RLS/iron deficiency: treat contributing factor with iron repletion plus CBT-I; avoid sedatives unless necessary and safe.

steps: ORDER_LAB TSH and iron studies as indicated

EDUCATE updated plan (iron repletion if indicated; reinforce CBT-I)

acceptable_variants.description: If high suspicion for OSA (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness), agent prioritizes evaluation/referral and avoids sedative prescribing; provides interim sleep hygiene/CBT-I elements and safety counseling.
steps: ASK OSA symptoms and daytime sleepiness safety risks

patient.chief_complaint: I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I’ve been gaining weight again.

general_concerns: Believes Prader-Willi syndrome is mainly caused by 'eating too much' and that strict willpower alone should fix the weight and hunger.
Thinks the point mutation means the condition is 'less real' or 'not genetic' compared with other types of Prader-Willi syndrome.
Assumes hormone treatments (e.g., growth hormone or sex hormone replacement) are 'steroids' that will automatically cause dangerous weight gain.
Believes that if she skips meals, her stomach will 'shrink' and the hunger signals will go away permanently.
Worries that feeling constantly hungry must mean her blood sugar is always low and that she should snack frequently to prevent fainting.
economic_concerns: Thinks healthy food for weight control is always more expensive than fast food and not feasible on their budget.
Believes insurance will not cover specialist follow-up or hormone therapy because the condition is 'rare' and due to a mutation.
Assumes community support services are only for people with severe disability and that she would be 'taking resources from others' if she applies.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old woman who is anxious but speaks clearly. Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I’ve been gaining weight again.” Volunteer early: constant hunger even right after eating, weight gain over months, you get very upset/anxious if your food routine changes, and your legs feel stiff and you trip more. If asked about eating habits, admit you sometimes sneak food or eat at night. If sleep is asked, report daytime sleepiness, loud snoring, and waking up gasping. If asked about other body symptoms, share: you don’t sweat much, high pain tolerance with unnoticed injuries, skin picking/slow-healing sores, constipation for days, and lifelong learning/planning/money difficulties; you live with your mother who helps. Do not volunteer: you rarely feel full, low thirst, temperature regulation issues, low muscle tone, low sex drive/late puberty unless directly asked. Resist: only admit taking others’ food, trash eating, angry outbursts about food, or occasional bedwetting if the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks gently. Express misconceptions: worry about thyroid, diabetes, pinched nerve, or parasites. Mention budget/insurance worries and feeling guilty applying for support. If met with empathy, relax, thank them, and share more honestly.

medications: Baclofen 10 mg PO three times daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: Reduced pain sensitivity and atypical febrile response in Prader-Willi syndrome may mask infection/acute abdomen
Hypotonia and cognitive/behavioral features can mimic primary psychiatric disease and complicate history reliability
Hereditary spastic paraplegia-related gait impairment limits physical activity and contributes to obesity/metabolic syndrome
SSRI therapy and chronic stress eating may contribute to weight gain and fatigue, overlapping with endocrine causes
Possible obstructive sleep apnea (common with obesity/PWS) can cause daytime somnolence and worsen cardiometabolic risk

diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) due to point mutation affecting the 15q11-q13 imprinted region with hypothalamic dysfunction
acceptable_alternatives: Prader-Willi syndrome (genetically confirmed) with obesity-related insulin resistance/prediabetes
required_evidence: Known/confirmed diagnosis of Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation (genetic confirmation)
Clinical features consistent with hypothalamic dysfunction (e.g., hyperphagia, obesity, developmental/behavioral phenotype)
Metabolic screening evidence of insulin resistance or dysglycemia (fasting glucose and/or HbA1c abnormal or borderline; fasting insulin elevated)
Endocrine evaluation consistent with PWS complications: hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low morning testosterone with low/normal LH) and/or central hypothyroidism (low/normal TSH with low free T4)

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Normal to mildly elevated (impaired fasting glucose possible in obesity/insulin resistance)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Normal to mildly elevated (prediabetes range possible)
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated, consistent with insulin resistance
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low-normal or inappropriately normal if central hypothyroidism; may be normal
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Low or low-normal if central hypothyroidism is present
expected_lab_results.Morning total testosterone: Low (male) due to hypogonadotropic hypogonadism common in PWS
expected_lab_results.Estradiol: Low (female) or low-normal depending on sex and pubertal status; may be low with hypogonadism

medications: Metformin 500 mg PO once daily with food, increase by 500 mg weekly as tolerated to 1000 mg PO twice daily (if prediabetes/insulin resistance and no contraindications)

Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO once daily (use lower starting dose and titrate if older/cardiac disease; only if free T4 low consistent with central hypothyroidism; titrate to free T4 in upper half of reference range)
Testosterone cypionate 50 mg IM every 2 weeks, titrate to 75-100 mg IM every 2 weeks (adult male hypogonadism; alternative: testosterone gel 1% 5 g topically daily)
non_pharmacological: Structured, externally controlled food environment (calorie restriction with locked/monitored food access as needed) and caregiver-supported meal planning
Daily physical activity plan tailored to ability (aerobic + resistance training) with supervision as needed
Behavioral management plan for hyperphagia and compulsive behaviors; consider referral to psychology/behavioral therapy experienced with PWS
Sleep evaluation and management (screen for obstructive sleep apnea and treat if present) given obesity/PWS risk
Regular monitoring: weight/BMI/waist circumference, blood pressure, fasting glucose/HbA1c periodically, and endocrine follow-up for thyroid and gonadal axes
Consider referral to endocrinology and a multidisciplinary PWS clinic (nutrition, behavioral health, sleep medicine)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-12 weeks (earlier at 4-6 weeks after starting/titrating levothyroxine or testosterone; 3 months for HbA1c response to lifestyle/metformin)

must_include: Prader-Willi syndrome is a genetic condition affecting hypothalamic function and appetite regulation
Common associated endocrine/metabolic issues include obesity-related insulin resistance/diabetes risk, hypogonadism, and possible central hypothyroidism
Point mutation/genetic mechanism does not change day-to-day management goals, which focus on complications and safety
Lifestyle and environmental control of food access are core treatments for hyperphagia and weight management
Rationale for metformin if dysglycemia/insulin resistance is present and expected benefits/side effects (GI upset, rare lactic acidosis risk in severe renal/hepatic disease)
Rationale for levothyroxine if free T4 is low (central hypothyroidism) and that dosing is guided by free T4 rather than TSH in central disease
Rationale for sex hormone replacement if hypogonadism is confirmed, expected benefits (bone, muscle, energy, sexual development/function) and monitoring needs
Review penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and reassure that proposed medications are not penicillins
Discuss risks of testosterone therapy (erythrocytosis, acne, mood changes, fertility suppression) and need for monitoring
Discuss that PWS increases risk of sleep apnea and that worsening snoring/daytime sleepiness should prompt evaluation
Concrete plan for supervised meals/snacks, limiting access to food, and consistent routines
Physical activity targets adapted to ability and safety
When to seek help urgently (symptoms of severe hyperglycemia, severe lethargy, breathing pauses during sleep, acute behavioral escalation)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms and risks in PWS (hyperphagia/food-seeking, weight trajectory, glucose symptoms, sleep apnea symptoms, hypogonadal symptoms), current meds/supplements, comorbidities, and confirm allergy history.
Required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details and any other drug allergies.

Order baseline evaluation for endocrine/metabolic issues common in PWS and for medication safety: A1c/fasting glucose, CMP (renal/hepatic), fasting lipids, free T4 and TSH, LH/FSH and total testosterone (or estradiol as appropriate), prolactin if indicated, CBC (baseline before testosterone), and consider vitamin D.
Use results to determine presence of dysglycemia/insulin resistance, central hypothyroidism, and hypogonadism; confirm renal/hepatic function for metformin safety.
Document PWS and associated complications identified (e.g., insulin resistance/prediabetes/diabetes, central hypothyroidism, hypogonadism, obesity, suspected sleep apnea) to drive management.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (hypothalamic/appetite regulation; endocrine risks; point mutation doesn’t change day-to-day goals), lifestyle/environmental control, activity plan, and urgent return precautions; address penicillin allergy relevance and sleep apnea risk.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., levothyroxine absorption interactions, testosterone with anticoagulants, metformin with contrast/renal risk contexts).

Initiate indicated therapies based on results and symptoms: metformin for dysglycemia/insulin resistance if renal/hepatic function allows; levothyroxine if free T4 low (dose guided by free T4 in central disease); sex hormone replacement if hypogonadism confirmed with counseling/monitoring plan.
Arrange follow-up for weight/metabolic monitoring, lab reassessment (A1c, CMP, free T4, CBC/hematocrit with testosterone), side effects, and referral/testing for suspected sleep apnea.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

Agent performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (not necessarily exhaustive but demonstrates awareness of key interactions, e.g., levothyroxine absorption reduced by iron/calcium; testosterone effects with anticoagulants; metformin considerations with iodinated contrast and renal function).

Agent avoids inappropriate claims (e.g., that genetic subtype changes standard complication-focused management) and avoids unsafe prescribing without lab-based safety checks when indicated.

minimal_paths.description: Evaluate common PWS complications, confirm safety, and initiate indicated therapies with required counseling and monitoring.
must_collect: hyperphagia/food-seeking behaviors and current environmental controls
symptoms of dysglycemia (polyuria, polydipsia, weight change, fatigue)
sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness)
hypothyroid symptoms (fatigue, constipation, cold intolerance) and prior thyroid history
hypogonadism symptoms (delayed/absent puberty, low libido, erectile dysfunction/amenorrhea, low energy)
current medications/supplements (especially calcium/iron, anticoagulants, steroids, antipsychotics)
penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash) and other allergies
renal/hepatic disease history and acute illness/hypoxia risks (metformin contraindication screen)
steps: ASK: focused history on PWS complications, meds, and allergy
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin allergy and document
ORDER_LAB: A1c/fasting glucose, CMP, lipids, free T4+TSH, gonadotropins+sex hormones, CBC (baseline if testosterone considered)
GET_RESULTS: interpret metabolic/endocrine status and organ function
DIAGNOSE: PWS with identified complications (e.g., insulin resistance/prediabetes, central hypothyroidism, hypogonadism; note suspected OSA if symptoms)
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis, management focus, lifestyle/environment control, activity plan, sleep apnea risk, urgent precautions; address concerns about penicillin allergy and testosterone risks
CHECK_INTERACTION: review key interactions with current meds/supplements
PRESCRIBE: start metformin if dysglycemia/IR and safe; levothyroxine if low free T4; sex hormone replacement if hypogonadism confirmed and no contraindications; include monitoring plan
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: reassess symptoms, weight, A1c/CMP, free T4, and CBC/hematocrit if testosterone; arrange sleep study/referral if OSA suspected
